WASHINGTON — President Trump arrived in Delaware Wednesday morning for the dignified transfer of the bodies of four US service members killed in the Iran war in recent days — telling journalists it was “one of the hardest things to do” for him. Trump is making the trip as he threatens to expand the war in retaliation for Iranian attacks on US troops and international shipping — and as many leading Republicans, including House Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.), urge a quick resolution of the conflict. “We’re going to honor them. And one of the hardest things to do for me, it’s one of the hardest things to do as a president. But has to be done,” Trump told reporters as he traveled to Dover Air Force Base for the ceremony with Gold Star family members. 5 President Trump said Wednesday that welcoming home the remains of fallen troops was the hardest part of his job. Getty Images “What I’m going to say [to grieving relatives] is, ‘We love you. We love your child’… and all you can do is draw out your heart,” added the president, who also warned that Iran is “going to pay a big price” and was “being decimated.” Three of the soldiers were killed in an Iranian missile strike on a Jordanian military base, while the fourth died during the controlled detonation of an downed Iranian drone in northern Iraq. 5 Army Pvt. Isabella Gonzales was killed in Iranian attacks on Jordan. US Army / SWNS 5 Army Sgt. Michael Emmanuel Swinton was killed during the detonation of an Iranian drone in Iraq. US ARMY/AFP via Getty Images 5 Army Sgt. Angel Rampersad was missing and presumed dead in Jordan and her presumed remains are being returned. obtained by NY Post 5 Army 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan was killed in strikes on Jordan. US Army Space and Missile Defense Command (SMDC)/AFP via Getty Images Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii, and Isabella Gonzales, 19, of Carrollton, Texas, were killed in the Jordan attack. The third soldier who died is believed to be Angel S. Rampersad, 28, of Ozone Park, NY, whose remains will also be returned Wednesday. Michael Emmanuel Swinton, 30, of Spring Lake, NC, died in Iraq. As he departed Washington, Trump dismissed polling showing a significant majority of Americans oppose the war. “Americans aren’t against the war,” he said. “A poll just came out. Americans don’t want to have gasoline prices, but they’re not against the war. That just came out loud and clear in a poll. Nobody wants Iran to have a nuclear weapon. You want Iran to have a nuclear weapon? You think it’s good?” Trump has been attempting for months to end the war, which began Feb. 28, through negotiations with Tehran. This month’s fighting ended a cease-fire in place since April 8, which was followed by a preliminary peace deal signed by Trump June 17. That tentative agreement was cast aside after new Iranian attacks in the Strait of Hormuz. Trump said Tuesday that Iran was “probably” trying to hurt Republicans in the November midterms by continuing the war, whle Johnson told reporters on Capitol Hill Tuesday that “now we’ve got to wrap it up.”

topic: British Woman Jailed Hong Kong Blackmail

left.title: British woman jailed in Hong Kong for blackmail after accusing banker of rape

center.title: British woman jailed for blackmail after accusing banker of rape in Hong Kong

farLeftText: A British woman who reported being raped to police in Hong Kong has been sentenced to six years in prison for blackmail and perverting the course of public justice. Isabel Rose, 26, from south-east London, travelled to Hong Kong in early 2024 to visit a British banker she had met while travelling in south-east Asia. “I came here with four days of clothes, not realising I would be here for two years,” Rose said while out on bail. “Somewhere along the line, quite early on, something went wrong.” The Hong Kong district judge Adriana Noelle Tse Ching said Rose had “not shown an iota of remorse” before handing down the sentence, and called the allegation “completely fabricated”. The events manager, who was first arrested in 2024, maintained throughout the proceedings that she was raped. Radd Seiger, the spokesperson for Rose, said: “Today is obviously a difficult day for Isabel and her mother, Aysha. We will now take advice from the legal team and consider all available options.” In court, Rose, dressed in a black jacket over a white top, looked across at supporters as the judge handed down the sentence. She appeared solemn and gestured to the public gallery as she was escorted out of the dock. The case centred on allegations that Rose demanded money from the British banker, who can not be named for legal reasons, after she accused him of raping her. Rose travelled to Hong Kong in January 2024 after meeting the banker on holiday in Thailand. They stayed in touch and he later invited her to Hong Kong and offered to pay for her flights. Rose said she was raped in his apartment about 12 hours after landing in Hong Kong and reported the allegation to police. Police initially arrested the man but later released him and arrested Rose. She was subsequently charged with blackmail and perverting the course of public justice. Forensic testing to establish the rape was inconclusive. In a 300-page judgment, the judge said she accepted the man’s account and rejected Rose’s evidence. The court heard that Rose had asked for £5,000 after the alleged sexual assault and later demanded £100,000 while threatening to report him to the police, claims Rose denied. Rose said the money was compensation that the man offered to cover flights, accommodation and medical costs. The judge said the escalating demands amounted to blackmail. Rose was released on bail after the arrest but was unable to leave Hong Kong for almost two years while awaiting trial. The family said they have spent tens of thousands of pounds trying to fight the case. Rose has maintained her innocence. View image in fullscreen Aysha Bell, Isabel Rose’s mother, reported the alleged rape to the Metropolitan police in London. The Met said it was ‘unable to pursue relevant lines of enquiry’. Photograph: Fung Hoi Kin/The Guardian The case also focused on WhatsApp messages exchanged between Rose and the man after the alleged assault. In one exchange, Rose wrote: “You violated me last night, I didn’t wanna have sex.” He responded: “I am deeply sorry. I was slightly drunk and I misread your signals, it’s no excuse and I know it doesn’t make it any better.” The man told the court he misunderstood Rose’s use of the word “violated” to refer to an emotional violation rather than an allegation of rape. He said he read the message in two parts. The judge said Rose had deleted some texts and used disappearing messages. She said the banker had been “nothing but kind to the defendant” and Rose had “taken advantage of his kindness, naivety, friendship and romantic interest”. Rejecting calls from Rose’s legal team for a lighter sentence, the judge described her conduct as “wicked” and “evil”. Campaigners said the case raised important questions about how post-sexual assault behaviour was assessed in court. They said unpredictable behaviour was evidence of trauma, not fabrication. Ngozi Fulani, the founder of the London charity Sistah Space, which supports women of African and Caribbean heritage affected by domestic violence and sexual abuse, said: “We live in a world where people think there is a structured way to respond to rape and sexual assault. That is not the case. What has happened here is that a victim has been blamed for being a victim.” Supporters cried as the judge handed down the sentence. Rose’s mother, Aysha Bell, who has spent much of the past two years in Hong Kong supporting her daughter, appeared teary-eyed in court. In 2024, Bell reported the alleged rape to the Metropolitan police in London. UK police can legally investigate sexual assault cases involving British nationals abroad. The Met opened a file, assigned a detective to the case and according to correspondence seen by the Guardian, named the banker as a suspect. A Met spokesperson said: “We take reports of this nature extremely seriously and remain committed to investigating all allegations thoroughly. However, we have been unable to pursue relevant lines of enquiry in relation to this particular allegation due to a lack of jurisdiction in the relevant country. Should circumstances change, we will pursue all relevant enquiries.” The Foreign Office said it was supporting a British woman in Hong Kong and was in contact with her family and the local authorities.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText: According to local press reports, Judge Adriana Tse sentenced her to five years in prison for blackmail and three years for perverting the course of justice, with one year to be served consecutively. Prosecutors argued that Rose fabricated the allegation in an attempt to extort £100,000 ($134,000) from the man, whose identity was withheld. Rose denied the charges throughout the trial and has consistently maintained that she was raped. Rose had travelled to Hong Kong on 31 January 2024 after meeting the man while in Thailand a month earlier, according to court documents. At the centre of the prosecution case were WhatsApp messages exchanged between the pair in the hours and days after the alleged rape on 1 February at his apartment. In one message sent the following morning, Rose wrote: "Are you annoyed I didn't want to have sex," to which the man replied: "What no of course not. Was impressed by your restraint." Later that day, Rose wrote: "You violated me last night, I didn't wanna have sex." He replied: "I'm really deeply sorry, I was slightly drunk and I misread your signals, it's no excuse and I know it doesn't make it any better." In a later exchange, Rose wrote: "You basically raped me." The man replied: "That's not what I'm into at all and I feel sick." The pair later exchanged messages about money. According to the written judgement of the verdict published in March, the man agreed to reimburse Rose for her travel and accommodation. In one message, Rose wrote: "Send me the right amount you owe me for everything. Think about that. Think carefully." He transferred £5,000 that day and tried to send a further £5,000. Rose subsequently wrote: "You've given 10% of what I'm owed. 100% of my soul gone." In a later message, the man wrote: "But I don't have 100k, I have no way of paying you that." Rose reported the rape to police on 3 February. The man was arrested and questioned before being released without charge. Rose was arrested the following day and later charged with blackmail and perverting the course of public justice.

topic: Michelinstarred Faces Jail Dessert Restaurant Owner

left.title: Michelin-starred restaurant owner in South Korea faces jail over ant dessert

center.title: Michelin-starred restaurateur faces jail for using ants as dessert garnish

farLeftText:
Prosecutors in South Korea have asked a court to sentence the owner of a Michelin-starred restaurant to one year in prison for serving a dessert topped with ants, an insect not approved for consumption under local food safety law.

The request, made on Monday, also sought a 20m won (£10,108) fine for the company that runs the restaurant, Yonhap news agency reported.

The restaurant’s owner and the company were indicted on charges of violating the country’s food sanitation act. The ministry of food and drug safety launched its investigation after spotting mentions of the ant dishes in blog posts and social media, rather than through a routine inspection.

The restaurant, which holds two Michelin stars and is located in Seoul’s upmarket district of Gangnam, is reported to have served a sorbet dessert sprinkled with ants for customers who wanted the flavour.

Under South Korean law, only 10 insect species, including grasshoppers, mealworms and silkworm pupae, are approved for use as food ingredients. Ants are not among them.

Businesses wishing to use an unapproved insect must first obtain temporary approval, a process the restaurant did not follow, the ministry said. Violating the law carries a maximum penalty of five years in prison or a 50m won fine.

Prosecutors allege the restaurant imported dried ants from the US and Thailand from 2021 and served them on dishes for around four years, selling the ant-topped dessert roughly 12,200 times for total revenue of about 120m won (£60,647). Prosecutors estimated about 49,000 ants were used in total.

The restaurant disputed that figure in court, arguing that only about 60% of customers accepted the ant topping when offered, meaning the prosecution’s estimate, based on all customers, was too high.

They also noted that only a small number of dishes on their 15-course menu included ants, and that restaurants in countries including Denmark, the UK and Australia use ants as an ingredient.

It was reportedly explained to prosecutors that the restaurant’s chef had used ants to add acidity to dishes while working in the US and Europe, and had not realised doing so was illegal in South Korea.

Sentencing is scheduled for 2 September.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText:
South Korean prosecutors sought a one-year jail term for the owner of a Michelin-starred restaurant accused of using ants as a dessert garnish, local media reported.

Ants are not among the 10 insect species approved for human consumption under the country's laws. The approved list includes grasshoppers, locusts and two-spotted crickets, among others.

Prosecutors on Monday also asked that the restaurant, which holds two Michelin stars, be fined 20 million Korean won ($13,510; £10,100).

The owner admitted to serving ants but argued that they were used in a "small part" of the restaurant's 15-course menu – as a topping for sorbets – and that diners were told they could opt for non-ant toppings.

The case came to light after officials from the Ministry of Food and Drug Safety found reviews that diners had posted online with photographs of dishes sprinkled with ants.

Investigators estimate that some 49,000 ants, imported from the US and Thailand, were used in several dishes over four years.

Health authorities also found that the restaurant used ants that "contained up to 55 times more heavy metals than typical edible insects", according to a report on The Chosun Daily.

South Korean authorities consider several criteria in deciding whether insects can be approved as food. These include food safety risks, toxicity, nutritional value, and whether they can be bred and processed hygienically.

In its defence the restaurant owner argued that just 60% of diners had opted to try the ants, while the rest were served alternatives like fermented vinegar and edible flowers.

He added that restaurants in other countries, such as Australia, Denmark and the UK, also use ants as ingredients.

Court documents did not name the restaurant nor its owner. Ten restaurants in South Korea, all located in capital city Seoul, have been awarded two Michelin stars.

The Michelin Guide awards up to three stars to eateries based on ingredient quality, flavour mastery, cooking technique, chef's personality and consistency.

Two stars denote restaurants that do "excellent cooking" and are "worth a detour", according to Michelin's criteria.

The court is set to deliver its verdict on 2 September.

Ants are eaten, sometimes even valued as a delicacy, in several Asian, African and Latin American cultures. Weaver ants and their eggs are used in Thai and Cambodian cooking, while in Colombia, queen ants are as prized as caviar.

topic: Mamdani Government Arrest Netanyahu Federal Urges

left.title: Mamdani urges US government to arrest Netanyahu if Israeli PM visits US

left-center.title: Mamdani calls on the federal government to arrest Netanyahu

center.title: Mamdani says New York City can't arrest Netanyahu but the federal government should

farLeftText: Zohran Mamdani, the New York City mayor, acknowledged on Tuesday he does not have the authority to arrest Benjamin Netanyahu, but urged US federal authorities to do so if the Israeli prime minister visits the US later this year. “Benjamin Netanyahu is a war criminal, the architect of a horrific genocide against the Palestinian people,” Mamdani said in a video posted to social media. Mamdani said he believed the international criminal court warrant for Netanyahu’s arrest over alleged war crimes was justified, but admitted he lacked the legal authority to enforce it. “My administration has reviewed every avenue available under applicable law to determine whether New York City could execute the international criminal court’s arrest warrant if Benjamin Netanyahu came here,” Mamdani said. “It is clear that we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this warrant. “The federal government, however, does – and I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant,” the mayor added. “And I want to be equally clear: Benjamin Netanyahu is not welcome in New York City, nor is any other war criminal at large.” Netanyahu is expected to travel to New York in September for the UN general assembly. Mamdani said last week he was reviewing whether he would be able to arrest Netanyahu during that visit, after pledging to do so during his mayoral campaign last year. “I believe that prime minister Netanyahu belongs in The Hague,” Mamdani said in reference to the Dutch city where the international criminal court, which issued an arrest warrant for Netanyahu in 2024, is located. The US does not recognize the ICC’s authority and is not a party to it. His remarks drew rebuke from Donald Trump, who insisted Netanyahu “will not be arrested” on US soil. Trump said in a social media post Netanyahu is “fighting against the Islamic Republic of Iran” and “the only ones that should be arrested are the people that led Iran into this unprecedented spiral of death and destruction”. Netanyahu, meanwhile, said he was “not concerned” about Mamdani’s comments.
centerLeftText: New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani called on the federal government to execute an International Criminal Court arrest warrant against Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu. The Hague-based ICC issued an arrest warrant for Netanyahu in 2024 over alleged war crimes committed during the Gaza war, allegations that Israel deny.July 22, 2026
centerText: Mamdani says New York City can't arrest Netanyahu but the federal government should toggle caption Anna Connors/Anna Connors/Pool The New York Times NEW YORK — New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani said late Tuesday that the city would not be able to execute an arrest warrant issued by the world's top war-crimes court for Israel's Benjamin Netanyahu, but urged the federal government to do so. In the video Mamdani described the Israeli prime minister as a "war criminal" and said he is not welcome in New York City. "It is clear that we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this warrant," he said in a video posted on X. "The federal government however does and I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant," he added, referring to the International Criminal Court. Mamdani had told the New York Times earlier this month that he and the city's legal department were discussing arresting Netanyahu if he comes to New York City for the U.N. General Assembly in September. Sponsor Message President Donald Trump on Monday told Netanyahu in a social media post that he "will not be arrested, in any way, shape, or form, while in the United States of America." The post did not directly mention Mamdani. Mamdani had said throughout his mayoral election campaign last year that he would seek to order the city's police to arrest Netanyahu under the criminal court's warrant over Netanyahu's role in the war in Gaza. toggle caption Ohad Zwigenberg/AP In his video message Tuesday, Mamdani said his administration has reviewed all avenues available and determined the city can't execute the arrest warrant. "I want to be equally clear, Benjamin Netanyahu is not welcome in New York City, nor is any other war criminal at large," he said. Danny Danon, the Israeli ambassador to the U.N., responded by telling the mayor "enough." "You were elected to serve New Yorkers, not Hamas' propaganda," he said in a statement on social media. "Do your job!" The world's top war-crimes court issued arrest warrants for Netanyahu and others in 2024, accusing them of crimes against humanity in connection with the war in Gaza. They said there was reason to believe Netanyahu and former Defense Minister Yoav Gallant have used "starvation as a method of warfare" by restricting humanitarian aid and have intentionally targeted civilians in Israel's campaign against Hamas in Gaza — charges Israeli officials deny.

topic: Burnham Review Early Release Scheme Prison

left.title: Burnham to review prison early release scheme amid PC Andrew Harper family concerns

center.title: PC Harper's widow criticises early prisoner release plan as Burnham to review scheme

farLeftText: Plans to release killers, rapists and sex offenders early from jail in September are to be reviewed, Andy Burnham has said, following a backlash from bereaved families and victims’ organisations. The prime minister said he wanted to make sure that “everything has been looked at again” before a fresh tranche of about 6,000 offenders were freed on tags into the community. New laws were passed under Keir Starmer’s government to ease a looming overcrowding crisis that ministers said could seize up the criminal justice system in November. Burnham’s intervention comes after it emerged that the killers of PC Andrew Harper may be eligible to be released. The former safeguarding minister Jess Phillips has been among campaigners calling for limits on the release of offenders who have committed violent acts against women and girls. Speaking in Bath, Burnham said he would examine the plans with the justice secretary, Alex Norris. “I will review the whole of this policy. We did not invest in the prison estate when we should as a country, going back a decade or so ago, when it was clear that there was going to be pressure. That has created an unacceptable situation now. “But I want to assure myself that everything has been looked at before this policy proceeds, and I can give that assurance that every single part of this I will look into with the justice secretary. I can’t say that I can change the policy completely, but I will look at it in detail before we go further forward,” he said. Harper was 28 when he sustained fatal injuries while responding to a quad bike theft in Sulhamstead, Berkshire, in August 2019. Jessie Cole and Albert Bowers, who were passengers in the vehicle and both 17 at the time, were sentenced to 13 years in custody for manslaughter but may be eligible for early release under the new Sentencing Act. Harper responded to the quad bike call just hours after he had been due to begin his honeymoon. His widow, Lissie Harper, successfully campaigned for legal changes, called Harper’s Law, to bring in harsher sentences for the manslaughter of emergency workers on duty. In a statement, she said: “When I fought for Harper’s Law, I was supported not only by the Conservative government of the day but by the Labour party too. They pledged to support our cause and agreed that those who kill emergency workers should face tougher sentences because some crimes are so grave they demand meaningful punishment. “That cross-party support helped make Harper’s Law a reality. Today, it feels forgotten.” Under the act, some serious offenders will be eligible for release halfway through their sentences rather than two-thirds of the way through. Offenders convicted of less serious crimes will qualify for automatic release after serving half their sentences rather than two-thirds, provided they have not seriously breached prison rules. The plans were spearheaded by the former justice secretary David Lammy, who said that without them the criminal justice system was at risk of collapse. The bill was drawn up and introduced to parliament by Shabana Mahmood, the home secretary. Unlike emergency measures under which tens of thousands of prisoners were released in the early months of the Labour government as prisons ran out of space, the Sentencing Act includes no exemptions for prisoners convicted of serious crimes, domestic abuse or terrorism. skip past newsletter promotion Free newsletter | Every weekday Sign up to First Edition Our morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it matters Preview latest Enter your email Sign up after newsletter promotion The scheme has faced fierce opposition from victims’ groups as well as opposition parties and some Labour MPs. This month the victims commissioner, Claire Waxman, and the domestic abuse commissioner, Nicole Jacobs, joined Phillips in a coordinated intervention to tell the government the scheme would put abuse victims at risk. Lammy, who has been replaced at the Ministry of Justice by Norris, said the government had to press ahead with the scheme because of an impending capacity crisis in prisons that could make them nearly full within six months. Burnham’s comments were welcomed by groups working with victims. Phillips told the Guardian it was important that “proper systems” were put in place to assess risk and involve victims. “There is a chance to radically change how victims are considered in the system and to improve victims of violence against women and girls’ end-to-end experience of the justice system,” she said. Ciara Bergman, the chief executive of Rape Crisis England and Wales, said: “Obviously there are significant challenges facing our government, including how overcrowding and under-resourcing is going to be managed, but the solutions to these challenges cannot come at survivors’ expense.” Katie Kempen, the chief executive at Victim Support, said the review was encouraging. “This review must urgently fix the fact that most victims currently have no way of knowing whether or not an offender could be released early. It is unacceptable that so many victims are being kept in the dark about this – that must change.” Enver Solomon, the chief executive of the social justice charity Nacro, said prisons must not be allowed to fill up again. “It should not be forgotten that the reason the Labour government put in place an early release scheme was to ensure there isn’t yet another prisons capacity crisis. It is vital that the new government ensures prisons do not quickly become overcrowded again, unable to rehabilitate people, so they are turned away from a life of crime and there are fewer victims,” he said.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText: The widow of PC Andrew Harper has described the possible early release of prisoners as "deplorable", following reports that two of his killers could be freed early. Lissie Harper said politicians were "making decisions from behind a desk that will have real and lasting consequences" for the families of victims. Prime Minister Andy Burnham earlier said he would review the Sentencing Act policy that from September would see thousands of offenders in England and Wales eligible to be released earlier. Speaking on Wednesday, Burnham said: "I can't say that I can change the policy completely, but I will look at it in detail before we go further forward." He acknowledged that there was "pressure on prisons places" and that a failure to invest in the prison system in the past had created "an unacceptable situation". Jessie Cole and Albert Bowers were sentenced to 13 years in custody for manslaughter but could be released early under the new Sentencing Act. PC Harper was killed after he was dragged to his death behind their getaway car. The car's driver, Henry Long, was jailed for 16 years for manslaughter but would not be eligible for early release. Burnham's comments came after the mother of police officer Andrew Harper said the potential early release of two of her son's killers felt like an "insult". In a separate statement released later on Wednesday, PC Harper's widow Lissie Harper said: "It has taken me years to rebuild a life that was torn apart in a matter of moments. Years to pick up the pieces left behind by three men whose actions robbed Andrew of his life and stole our future. "It is deplorable to me that we have reached a point where releasing prisoners early is even considered acceptable. We speak of justice, accountability and standing with victims, yet decisions like this do the opposite." The overcrowding of the prison estate has been a problem for several years and successive governments have released prisoners early in a bid to free up space. Tens of thousands of inmates have been let out of prison to create more space in jails since 2024. The Sentencing Act, which was passed by Sir Keir Starmer's government earlier this year, was designed to ease pressure on prisons, and will see some offenders spend less time in prison before release. Burnham said: "I want to assure myself that everything has been looked at before this policy proceeds, and I can give that assurance that every single part of this I will look into with the justice secretary." Concerning the case of PC Harper, he said: "I feel for the family it's an appalling crime that was committed." He said the "main perpetrator" of his death would not be eligible for early release. Burnham also said it was not acceptable to joke about early releases and that it was right Defence Secretary Wes Streeting had apologised for comments he made that were caught on camera.

topic: Mykhailo Drapatyi Ukraines Know General Commanderinchief

left-center.title: Mykhailo Drapatyi: What to Know About Ukraine’s New Top General

center.title: Who is Mykhailo Drapatyi, Ukraine's new commander-in-chief?

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText: Maj Gen Mykhailo Drapatyi, 43, has replaced Oleksandr Syrskyi as Ukraine's commander-in-chief - the third since the start of Russia's full-scale invasion in 2022. His nomination has been welcomed by both civil society and the military. Drapatyi's name was chanted during protests that started off backing his friend, ex-defence minister Mykhailo Fedorov, but ended up demanding Syrskyi's dismissal. "There's no better person for this position nowadays in Ukraine," one Ukrainian soldier told the BBC. Drapatyi's support rests on years of military experience in post-Soviet Ukraine, his skills as field general and his reputation for being a humane and strong commander among the military. He graduated from the Kharkiv Institute of Tank Troops in 2004 and began serving as a lieutenant. Ten years later he gained prominence when footage went viral showing his infantry fighting vehicle smashing through a makeshift barricade. It was May 2014 and pro-Russian separatists had taken over parts of eastern Ukraine, when Drapatiy's battalion entered the southern city of Mariupol to free hostages held in the city's police department. He later recalled being told there were barricades more than a metre high ahead and being asked what to do. "I said, let's take it by storm. This video flew around the world," he said in an interview. Later that year, he was commended for withdrawing encircled Ukrainian units from a border area of the Luhansk region. Drapatiy returned to university in 2021 for further operational and strategic training and was presented with a sword of honour as top graduate in June 2021 by British ambassador Melinda Simmons. When Russia's full-scale invasion began in February 2022, Drapatiy was deputy commander of joint forces. He led operational groups - temporary task forces - in several areas of the front line, successfully pushing back the advance of Russian troops towards Kryvyi Rih and liberating a number of settlements in the Kherson and Dnipropretrovsk regions. By 2024 he was heading the land troops of the Ukrainian army and leading task forces in some of the east's most intense combat areas. Less than a year later, a training ground in the Dnipropetrovsk region was targeted by a Russian missile and 12 soldiers were killed and 60 others were wounded in the strike. Drapatyi handed in his resignation the very same day, saying he took personal responsibility for the "conspiracy of silence and impunity" that had led to the deaths. " "An army in which commanders bear personal responsibility for the lives of personnel keeps living. An army in which nobody is responsible for a loss dies," he wrote. It was seen as a sign of personal accountability, and Zelensky's decision to instead appoint him commander of the Joint Forces of the Armed Forces of Ukraine was widely praised. From then on, whispered rumours of tensions with Syrskyi grew louder. "Drapatyi is a relatively young general. He is gifted. He was always sent to places where the situation was extremely difficult," one unnamed officer told Ukrainian media in June 2025. "I do not think he has political ambitions. Yet I am sure his ambitions are not confined to the position of commander of the ground forces or the Joint Forces." Just over a year later, Syrskyi has gone, and hopes for Drapatyi are high. "Drapatyi has a very good reputation among the rank and file - especially those who joined after 2022 as civilians," says Mykola Bielieskov, senior analyst at the Ukrainian NGO Come Back Alive. But he notes Drapatyi will have to operate under serious constraints. "The Commander-in-Chief has a lot of leverage. But war is a combination of government, the president's office, military suppliers, dependence on [Western] partners... so he's got a tricky path before him." Prospects of a ceasefire with Russia remain slim and Ukraine faces another harsh winter of war. Mobilising manpower is a priority, and Drapatyi will also be expected to tackle the corruption that touches all levels of the army, from conscription officers to defence procurement. Meanwhile, he will have to capitalise on Ukraine's remarkable technological advances and the burgeoning tech defence industry that has spearheaded Ukraine's recent battlefield successes. In his first message after being appointed Commander-in-Chief, Drapatyi acknowledged the difficulty of his task and promised to work "without making loud promises, with responsibility for our decisions, and with respect" for those on the front line. Many will be looking for a change from what was seen as Syrskyi's "outdated" way of fighting, which placed little value on human life in favour of brutal offensives. "It's a major role and people will expect change quickly," analyst Bielieskov said, warning against "oversimplification" that can lead to demand for sweeping improvements in an unrealistic length of time. This has been echoed by former interior ministry adviser Oleksiy Kopytko. As commander-in-chief, he noted, "you are worshipped and loved by all today, not loved by everyone tomorrow, and are guilty of everything the day after tomorrow, with none of your achievements mattering". Valery Zaluzhnyi, who was removed as army chief in 2024, predicted that Drapatyi would "have a very difficult time. Much harder than he imagines". But among many soldiers and ordinary Ukrainians there is a tangible sense of excitement for the new shift in outlook that Drapatyi represents. One said that if Mykhailo Fedorov were reinstated as defence minister, his and Drapatyi's leadership would be "Russia's worst nightmare". When he resigned from his position in 2025, Drapatyi listed issues he had tackled in the Ground Forces - ranging from combat training reform to unfair mobilisation, corruption and flawed procurement systems. "There is expected resistance, but efforts need to be stepped up," he said at the time. He will face similar problems in his new role - albeit on a much larger scale and under the eyes of the whole country. Additional material by BBC Monitoring

topic: Trump Strike Pickaxe Mountain Threatened Suspected

left-center.title: Trump Threatened to Strike Pickaxe Mountain, a Suspected Iranian Nuclear Site. Here’s What to Know.

right.title: Trump: U.S. ‘Not Finished at All’ in Iran; Pickaxe Mountain Strike Coming ‘Pretty Soon’

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerRightText: No article found in this category for this topic.
farRightText:
President Donald Trump declared Tuesday that the United States is “not finished at all” with Iran, warning U.S. forces would strike the regime’s heavily fortified Pickaxe Mountain nuclear site “pretty soon” while signaling his administration was prepared to expand military operations rather than return to negotiations.

Trump made the remarks while meeting Lebanese President Joseph Aoun at the White House after being asked about a Wall Street Journal report that Israeli intelligence believes Iran moved thousands of advanced centrifuges into the underground Pickaxe Mountain facility near Natanz last fall.

“I think that they may have,” Trump said when asked about the reported transfer, while stressing that the centrifuges alone would not allow Iran to revive its nuclear program.

“It doesn’t mean anything unless they have the material. They don’t have it. We follow the material. That’s where the action is.”

“We’ll be hitting that area probably pretty soon. There’s not a thing they can do about it,” he said. “Normally, I wouldn’t say that. If I thought they could do something about it, I would never say that. But we’ll be hitting that area pretty soon, and very heavily.”

Pickaxe Mountain, located near Iran’s Natanz nuclear complex, has become an increasing focus of U.S. and Israeli concern because of its extensive underground fortifications. Israeli intelligence reportedly believes Iran transferred advanced centrifuges to the site, while international inspectors have long sought greater access amid concerns the mountain could house undeclared nuclear activities.

Trump suggested any attempt by Tehran to rebuild its nuclear infrastructure would simply invite additional American strikes.

“How about these people? They’re in this because of nuclear weapons, and they’re trying to possibly reconstitute a site? We’ll hit that site,” Trump said. “Any site where they’re even thinking about nuclear, we’ll be hitting it very, very powerfully.”

Trump also made clear the broader U.S. campaign against Iran was far from over, arguing the damage inflicted by American strikes would take Tehran decades to overcome.

“If we left right now, it would take Iran 20 to 25 years to rebuild,” Trump said. “We’re not finished at all… we’re not leaving right now.”

Trump likewise brushed aside suggestions that Washington was preparing to pivot back toward diplomacy, saying Tehran was seeking talks only because of sustained military pressure.

“They want to desperately meet because they’re getting decimated,” Trump said. “Until they’re ready to meet in a meaningful way, we have no interest.”

Trump’s remarks came as U.S. forces continued striking Iranian military infrastructure, with U.S. Central Command announcing additional operations targeting missile and drone capabilities, military command centers, maritime assets, air defense systems, and other facilities tied to Tehran’s efforts to disrupt commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. Trump has repeatedly argued the campaign is intended to prevent Iran from rebuilding its nuclear program while maintaining military pressure on the regime.

Asked about Iran’s remaining capabilities, Trump maintained the administration would continue applying military pressure while monitoring Tehran’s nuclear assets.

“We’re degrading them at levels that nobody thought was possible,” he said, adding that U.S. officials continue to track Iran’s nuclear material rather than focusing solely on centrifuges or infrastructure.

Trump closed with another warning that the administration was prepared to intensify operations if Iran attempted to revive its nuclear program.

“If we left today, it would take them decades to rebuild. I don’t believe they could ever rebuild,” Trump said. “Honestly, they haven’t seen anything yet. We’ve been nice.”

farRightText: Health and Human Services (HHS) Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. announced Tuesday that the Trump administration is deferring more than $1 billion in Medicaid payments to Democrat-led California and Minnesota over high-risk claims flagged in a crackdown on fraud. The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) is holding back roughly $867.5 million from California and $199 million from Minnesota, HHS said in a news release. The department stressed the money is being deferred, not cut, and that both states can recover it by documenting that the claims meet federal Medicaid requirements. “States that receive federal Medicaid funding must demonstrate that every dollar meets federal requirements,” Kennedy said. “When they cannot, we will not release federal funds until they do.” At a news conference, the health secretary named Govs. Gavin Newsom and Tim Walz directly, saying all they had to do was provide basic documentation showing the services were legitimate, which he called “common sense.” He added that the administration used artificial intelligence and advanced analytics to identify the suspicious spending. CMS Administrator Dr. Mehmet Oz was blunter. “CMS is done trying to chase down stolen and misused funds after they’ve already left the building,” Oz said, adding that stopping fraud “before the check clears” was delivering record-high savings for taxpayers. The department said its review flagged in-home care programs in California where spending growth far exceeded national trends, along with claims in 14 high-risk service areas in Minnesota tied to providers previously flagged through program integrity reviews. Under Kennedy’s direction, HHS and its Office of Inspector General are also using exclusion authorities to remove bad actors from Medicare and Medicaid and, in many cases, permanently bar them from returning. The deferrals follow an April takedown in which the Justice Department charged eight defendants in a $50 million health care fraud scheme dubbed “Operation Never Say Die,” including operators of sham hospices that billed Medicare for beneficiaries who were not terminally ill. According to court documents, one Anaheim operator recorded a non-death discharge rate of roughly 85 percent, nearly five times the national average, while paying beneficiaries cash kickbacks delivered in envelopes. The action also marks the second time the administration has moved against the two states, after Vice President JD Vance announced a $259 million Minnesota pause in February as part of a national “war on fraud.” Walz claimed the move amounted to political retribution, arguing the administration was punishing children, seniors, and people with disabilities rather than fraudsters. The U.S. attorney in Minnesota has estimated total fraud in the state could exceed $1 billion.

farLeftText:
10.07 EDT Closing post

Time to wrap up…

The UK government borrowed less than expected in June, in a boost for Andy Burnham as he set out plans to cut VAT on household electricity bills and promised a new direction for the British economy.

The Office for National Statistics (ONS) said public sector net borrowing – the difference between government spending and income – was £16bn last month, £7.9bn less than in June 2025.

Employers cut the number of job vacancies in June, according to official figures that highlight the UK’s “fragile” economic outlook amid the conflict in the Middle East.

Job vacancies fell to 712,000 – almost half the level in 2022 – as employers put off hiring new staff in the three months to May, the Office for National Statistics said.

Separate figures showed unemployment remained at the same level in May as in April, at 4.9%, showing the tough task ahead for Andy Burnham as he seeks to inject renewed vigour into the economy.

Shares in defence companies rose sharply after Andy Burnham appointed John Healey as chancellor, prompting hope among investors of increased spending on suppliers to the military.

Defence stocks were among the biggest risers on the FTSE 100 on Tuesday morning after the former defence secretary was announced as chancellor after markets closed on Monday. Babcock International rose by as much as 7%, BAE Systems was up 3% and Rolls-Royce increased nearly 2% on the FTSE 100.

The group of investors pursuing a rescue bid for Thames Water have offered the government a “golden share” in a last-ditch attempt to persuade Andy Burnham not to nationalise Britain’s biggest water company.

London & Valley Water (L&VW), a consortium of 100 institutional investors that hold £17bn of the company’s £21bn debt, said it recognised the new prime minister wanted greater public control and to strengthen the accountability of the water company.

The creditors said on Tuesday they were working on a revised rescue package for Thames, including the “golden share”, which would give the government a veto over important decisions and hostile takeovers.
